steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has a complete main clause: "Congress designated a portion of Pennsylvania as the Lackawanna Valley National Heritage Area." After the comma, the blank introduces something that explains the reason for the designation. The question asks for the choice that follows Standard English conventions — no comma splice.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'they cite': adds a full subject ("they") and verb, creating a second independent clause joined to the first by only a comma — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — 'cited': a simple past verb with an implied subject ("Congress cited"), making another independent clause — again a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice C — 'citing': a present-participle phrase; it modifies the main clause and adds the reason without introducing a new subject or creating a second clause. No punctuation problem. ✓
  • Choice D — 'has cited': a present-perfect verb with an implied subject, producing a third independent clause and a comma splice. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
